content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 14, 1999


Dennis G. Plessas
2007 Granite Bar Way
Gold River, California 95670-8327

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. I-99-075

Dear Mr. Plessas:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You have provided insufficient facts for a definitive answer to your question. Therefore, we consider your letter to be a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).² Further, this letter is based on the facts as they have been presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact when issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May you participate, as a director for the Sacramento County Fire Protection District (the "district") in the governmental decision to reorganize the district with the American River Fire Protection District?

CONCLUSION

     You have not provided sufficient facts to answer this question. You are an employee of the American River Fire Protection District ("ARFPD"). Your salary from ARFPD is not disqualifying unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision to reorganize with the

___________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity granted by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

File No. I-99-075
Page 2

Sacramento Fire Protection District would have a personal financial effect of $250 or more
during a 12-month period.

FACTS

You are a newly elected director for the Sacramento County Fire Protection District.
As of March 15, 1999, the majority of the Sacramento County Fire Protection District's Board
(the "board") has formally approved discussing reorganization with the ARFPD.

You are currently employed as a division chief with the ARFPD. In this position, you
manage the data processing, communications, and community services divisions.

ANALYSIS

According to Section 87100, a public official is prohibited from making, participating in
making, or attempting to influence a governmental decision in which he or she has a financial
interest. Specifically, Section 87103 states that a public official has a financial interest in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of his or her economic interests.
In order to determine if you have a disqualifying conflict of interest, there is an eight-step
analysis, as set out in Regulation 18700(b), which will direct you to an answer.

1. Are you a Public Official?

A public official is any member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency, including, but not limited to, paid or unpaid members of boards or
commissions with decisionmaking authority. (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) Since you are
a director on the Sacramento County Fire Protection District Board, you are a public official
because you are a member of a local government board with decisionmaking authority.

2. Are you Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a Governmental Decision?

As a public official, you must next determine if you will be making, participating in
making, or attempting to use your official position to influence a governmental decision
according to Regulations 18702.1-18702.4. In your letter, you stated that the Sacramento County
Fire Protection District Board, of which you are a member, formally approved discussing
reorganization with the ARFPD. Unless an exception applies, if you are a part of that discussion,
you are making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.

File No. I-99-075
Page 3

3. What are your Economic Interests?

Next, you must identify your economic interests. If the governmental decision at issue
will have a foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests, you have a conflict.

There are six kinds of economic interests from which conflicts may arise. They are
defined in Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5 to include:

• An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect
investment³ of $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a), Regulation 18703.1(a).)

• An interest in a business entity in which a public official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d), Regulation
18703.1(b).)

• An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect interest of
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)

• Any source of income to the public official which aggregates to $250 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation 18703.3.)

• Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $300⁴ or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e), Regulation 18703.4.)

• A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family, a.k.a. the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.)

Based upon the facts you have presented in your request for advice, we can only infer two
possible economic interests, one in your income from your employer, the ARFPD, and the other
in your personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities or those of your immediate family. Be
advised that you may have additional economic interests (see list above) that may result in
conflicts of interest which we not discernable from the information contained in your request.

³ Section 87103 defines an 'indirect investment' or interest as any investment or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or
trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

⁴ The gift limit amount is adjusted biennially by the Commission to reflect changes in the Consumer Price
Index. As of January 1, 1999, the amount is $300. (Section 87103(e), Regulation 18940.2.)

File No. 1-99-075
Page 4

For example, if you own real property in the area of the Sacramento or American River Fire
Protection Districts, a decision on reorganization may affect the fair market value of your
property.

A. Salary from Government Not a Disqualifying Economic Interest Unless There is
a Personal Effect

An official is not disqualified from a governmental decision if the decision only affects
the official's salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses received from a state or local
government agency. (Section 82030(b)(2); Regulation 18705(c)(1); Cosgrove Advice Letter,
No. A-98-145.) Since the ARFPD is a local government agency, presumably any salary you
receive from the district would not be a disqualifying economic interest. (Cosgrove Advice
Letter, supra.) This is a very narrow and fact specific exception which would not apply to effects
on your personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities or those of your immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.)

B. Effect on Personal Expenses, Income, Assets, or Liabilities

If the decision will result in the increase or decrease of $250 or more in any 12-month
period on the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of you or your immediate family,
the personal effect is considered material and therefore a disqualifying conflict of interest.
(Regulation 18705.5.) Although your governmental salary would not generally be a problem,
whether the decision will have some other material personal effect is not something we can
analyze without more specific facts. For example, if the reorganization plan were to eliminate
your position, the exception would not apply and you would have to disqualify yourself.
(Daniels Advice Letter, No. 1-98-297, copy enclosed.)

At this point, the analysis of your salary as an economic interest ends since we have
determined that it falls within the governmental salary exception, and is therefore not a conflict.
Additionally, without further information, we cannot complete the analysis of the effect on your
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities. For your information, the remaining steps of the
analysis are outlined below.

4. Are your Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly Involved in the Governmental
Decision?

As a public official, you must determine if your economic interests will be directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision that you will be making, participating in
making, or influencing while in your official position as a director on the board. Regulation
18704 states that the economic interest must be material and the financial effect of the

File No. 1-99-075
Page 5

governmental decision must be foreseeable. Step five addresses materiality, and step six deals
with foreseeability.

5. Will the Effect of the Decision on your Economic Interest be Material?

The financial effect of a governmental decision is material if it will have a significant
effect on the public official, on a member of the official's immediate family, on the source of
income, the source of gifts, the business entity, or the real property. (Regulation 18705.)

6. Is the Material Effect Foreseeable?

If it is substantially likely that the governmental decision will have a direct or indirect
material effect on your economic interests, then the material effects are reasonably foreseeable.
(Regulation 18706.)

7. Will the Decision Affect the Public Generally?

This is commonly referred to as the "public generally exception." Even if a
governmental decision is found to have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
official's economic interest, the official will not be disqualified if the decision affects a
significant segment of the public generally in substantially the same manner. (Regulation
18707.)

8. Is your Participation in the Decision Legally Required?

If the public official's participation is legally required (as defined in Section 87101 and
Regulation 18708) despite the conflict of interest, then the official may participate in the
decision, but must disclose the potential conflicts of interest for the record. Participation is not
legally required if a quorum of other members can be assembled, or if the official is only needed
to break a tie.

File No. I-99-075
Page 6

I have enclosed some general materials regarding the conflict of interest rules, and copies
of the regulations referred to in this letter.5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Cynthia A. Schwerin
Legal Intern, Legal Division


SGC:CS:tls
Enclosures




5 Please be advised that the conflict of interest regulations were reorganized in October 1998. All
references in this letter are to the newly renumbered sections. See the Note in Regulation 18700 for the applicable
conversion chart.

Will the Effect of the Decision on your Economic Interest be Material?
The financial effect of a governmental decision is material if it will have a significant

I have enclosed some general materials regarding the conflict of interest rules, and copies

QUESTION: May you participate, as a director for the Sacramento County Fire Protection District (the
“district”) in the governmental decision to reorganize the district with the American River Fire
Protection District?

CONCLUSION: You have not provided sufficient facts to answer this question. You are an employee of
the American River Fire Protection District (“ARFPD”). Your salary from ARFPD is not
disqualif’ing unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision to reorganize with the
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code ofRegulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity granted by a Commission opinion
or formal. written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
Sacramento Fire Protection District would have a personal financial effect of $250 or more
during a 12-month period.
